What we witness in this chapter is not primarily a military defeat. Yes, Assyria's armies surrounded Samaria and carried away Israel's people. Yes, Hoshea proved himself a fool, trying to play both sides against Assyria and Egypt until Shalmaneser had had enough. But the narrator makes relentlessly clear that the real catastrophe was not geopolitical—it was spiritual. Israel fell because Israel had abandoned God. The armies were simply the instrument of a judgment that had been deserved long before the first siege began. This is what makes the chapter so unsettling: there is no injustice here, no tragedy striking an innocent people. There is only the inevitable harvest of persistent, willful rebellion.

The indictment against Israel is so specific, so detailed, that we cannot mistake what was actually happening in those towns and on those hills. They built high places in every city. They set up sacred poles and pillars. They burned incense just like the pagan nations God had driven out before them. They sacrificed their own children in fire. They practiced divination and soothsaying. They worshiped the host of heaven and served Baal. And they did all this while repeatedly hearing God's word through prophets who came with the same message: turn around, keep My commandments, remember the covenant. The word of God was not hidden from them. They simply would not listen. They stiffened their necks like their fathers, "becoming worthless" themselves as they pursued worthless idols. There is something almost physically repulsive in the picture the text gives us—a people so committed to spiritual corruption that they literally made cast idols of calves and worshiped them alongside the true God, as if the two could coexist, as if their sins could somehow be compartmentalized.

What happens next is what judgment actually looks like. God does not gently fade away or pretend the matter is unimportant. The text says plainly: "the LORD was very angry with Israel, and He removed them from His presence." The northern kingdom of Israel—the people who had seen God's deliverance, who had received His covenant at Sinai, who had been warned again and again—was scattered among the nations and effectively ceased to exist as a distinct people. God kept His covenant, and part of that covenant was that unfaithfulness would bring disaster. What is perhaps most chilling is the epilogue: the foreign peoples brought in to replace Israel eventually learned to fear the Lord out of self-interest—the lions were eating their people, after all—but they never abandoned their idols. They mixed their worship of God with worship of their own gods. They created a religion that was half-faith and half-rebellion, a comfortable compromise that honored both God and the nation they had come from. And we are told they "are still practicing their former customs" to this day. The people who lost their land refused to wholly follow the God who could have saved them. The people who replaced them refused to wholly abandon the gods who could not.

Stand here for a moment with what Scripture actually says: covenant-breaking has consequences. Persistent refusal to listen to God's word has consequences. The mixture of true worship with idolatry, the compartmentalizing of our loyalty so that we give God a place in our lives while reserving other loyalties for ourselves—this has consequences. We live in a time when such warnings seem quaint, when the idea that God actually judges sin strikes many as unloving and harsh. But the God of 2 Kings 17 is the same God who rules now. He has not changed His nature or His standard. His patience is real, but it is not infinite. His love is real, but it is not indifference to how we actually live. The question the chapter leaves us with is not a gentle one: Are you listening to what God has spoken? Or are you stiffening your neck like those who came before, pursuing your own path until there is nothing left?
